perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws. The Receiving Party and its Representatives: (a) irrevocably and unconditionally consent and submit to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the State of New York for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement: (b) agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to the address set forth at the end of this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives; (c) irrevocably and unconditionally waive any objection to the laying of venue of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement in any state or federal court located in the State of New York; and (d) irrevocably and unconditionally waive the right to plead or claim, and irrevocably and unconditionally agree not to plead or claim, that any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement that is brought in any state or federal court located in the State of New York has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to the laws of conflict of laws. The Receiving Party and its Representatives: (a) irrevocably and unconditionally consent and submit to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the State of Delaware for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement: (b) agree that service of any process, summons, notice or document by U.S. registered mail to the address set forth at the end of this Agreement shall be effective service of process for any action, suit or proceeding brought against the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives; (c) irrevocably and unconditionally waive any objection to the laying of venue of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement in any state or federal court located in the State of Delaware; and (d) irrevocably and unconditionally waive the right to plead or claim, and irrevocably and unconditionally agree not to plead or claim, that any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement that is brought in any state or federal court located in the State of Delaware has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to Delaware alters the rights and protections afforded under the contract. If the company's principal place of business and the underlying transaction have significant connections to New York, forcing litigation into Delaware may contradict legal precedents regarding choice of law and forum selection clauses, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under New York Law
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Changing the governing law and forum to Delaware, when the contract and parties have a significant nexus to New York, could be interpreted as a violation of this covenant if it demonstrably disadvantages one party and provides no reasonable justification other than to gain a tactical advantage in potential litigation. This could be viewed as an attempt to evade New York's established legal principles and procedures, which may be more favorable to one of the parties involved, creating a legal risk under New York law.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified New York law as governing the agreement, with jurisdiction and venue in New York. The changed text replaces this with Delaware. The Law Explanation suggests this change could violate New York's implied covenant of good faith if the contract and parties have a strong connection to New York, and the change disadvantages one party without reasonable justification. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2, both referencing UCC § 1-304, state that every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By changing the governing law to Delaware, if the circumstances outlined in the Law Explanation are met, the contract could be seen as attempting to evade New York law and potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith, creating a legal contradiction under New York law. This falls under 'Omission Legal Contradiction', as a legally mandated consumer protection (implied covenant of good faith) is effectively omitted or weakened by the change in governing law.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return or destroy, at its sole option, all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect to a substantial degree any Confidential Information. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 6, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, any return or destruction is subject to law, regulation and internal document retention policies.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect to a substantial degree any Confidential Information. Notwithstanding the return to the Company of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 6, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'destruction' option and the clause about document retention policies could force the Receiving Party to violate legal hold obligations if litigation is pending and the information is relevant. This creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 37(e) addresses the failure to preserve electronically stored information in anticipation or conduct of litigation. By removing the option to destroy confidential information upon request and omitting consideration for internal document retention policies, the modified contract language could compel a party to violate its legal duty to preserve potentially relevant information if a lawsuit is reasonably anticipated. This creates a direct conflict with federal rules governing discovery and spoliation of evidence, which could result in sanctions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions (a) Motion for an Order Compelling Disclosure or Discovery. (1) In General. On notice to other parties and all affected persons, a party may move for an order compelling disclosure or discovery. The motion must include a certification that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the person or party failing to make disclosure or discovery in an effort to obtain it without court action. (2) Appropriate Court. A motion for an order to a party must be made in the court where the action is pending. A motion for an order to a nonparty must be made in the court where the discovery is or will be taken. (3) Specific Motions. (A) To Compel Disclosure. If a party fails to make a disclosure required by Rule 26(a) , any other party may move to compel disclosure and for appropriate sanctions. (B) To Compel a Discovery Response. A party seeking discovery may move for an order compelling an answer, designation, production, or inspection. This motion may be made if: (i) a deponent fails to answer a question asked under Rule 30 or 31 ; (ii) a corporation or other entity fails to make a designation under Rule 30(b)(6) or 31(a)(4) ; (iii) a party fails to answer an interrogatory submitted under Rule 33 ; or (iv) a party fails to produce documents or fails to respond that inspection will be permitted—or fails to permit inspection—as requested under Rule 34 . (C) Related to a Deposition. When taking an oral deposition, the party asking a question may complete or adjourn the examination before moving for an order. (4) Evasive or Incomplete Disclosure, Answer, or Response. For purposes of this subdivision (a), an evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response must be treated as a failur

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the option to destroy confidential information and omits consideration for internal document retention policies. The Law Explanation states this could compel a party to violate its legal duty to preserve potentially relevant information if a lawsuit is reasonably anticipated, creating a direct conflict with federal rules governing discovery and spoliation of evidence. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing FRCP 37, discusses sanctions for failure to make disclosures or cooperate in discovery. By removing the option for destruction and omitting document retention policies, the modified contract increases the risk of violating discovery obligations, thus contradicting FRCP 37's aim to ensure cooperation in discovery and avoid sanctions for failing to preserve relevant information. The original text acknowledges these obligations, while the changed text does not.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that in the event of any breach of the terms of this Agreement, the other party may not be made whole by monetary damages only. Accordingly, each party, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek, at its sole expense, an injunction (which shall include a temporary restraining order) to prevent breaches of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that in the event of any breach of the terms of this Agreement, the other party may not be made whole by monetary damages only. Accordingly, each party shall be entitled to seek, at its sole expense, an injunction to prevent breaches of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or in equity' limits the remedies available to the parties, potentially violating the principle that parties should have access to all available legal remedies for breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3001
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR § 3001 allows for declaratory judgments, providing parties with a mechanism to resolve legal uncertainties and disputes. By removing access to 'any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or in equity', the amended language potentially restricts a party's ability to seek a declaratory judgment or other equitable relief in the event of a breach, thereby contradicting the broad remedial scope envisioned by New York law.

Declaratory judgment. The supreme court may render a declaratory judgment having the effect of a final judgment as to the rights and other legal relations of the parties to a justiciable controversy whether or not further relief is or could be claimed. If the court declines to render such a judgment it shall state its grounds. A party who has brought a claim for personal injury or wrongful death against another party may maintain a declaratory judgment action directly against the insurer of such other party, as provided in paragraph six of subsection (a) of section three thousand four hundred twenty of the insurance law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes "in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or in equity," thereby limiting the remedies available to the parties. The law explanation states this might restrict a party's ability to seek a declaratory judgment, contradicting the broad scope envisioned by New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CVP § 3001, states that "The supreme court may render a declaratory judgment having the effect of a final judgment as to the rights and other legal relations of the parties to a justiciable controversy whether or not further relief is or could be claimed." By removing the clause, the parties are prevented from seeking all other remedies available to them, creating a contradiction. The altered text makes the contract more restrictive than allowed under law, in violation of CVP § 3001.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws may prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities. Notwithstanding the foregoing or anything to the contrary in this Agreement, the Company acknowledges and agrees that this Agreement is in no way intended to restrict Receiving Party’s (or its Representatives’) ability to trade in securities or instruments (whether physical or derivative) of the Company or any of its affiliates or subsidiaries.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws may prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the acknowledgement that the agreement does *not* restrict securities trading could imply that the NDA *does* restrict trading, potentially misleading the Receiving Party and conflicting with insider trading regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. While an NDA can legitimately restrict the use of confidential information, removing the clause clarifying that the NDA *does not* restrict securities trading opens the door for misinterpretation. It could suggest that the NDA is intended to prevent legitimate trading activity, especially if the Confidential Information is material and non-public, thereby creating a potentially misleading impression that might violate securities laws if enforced in a way that improperly restricts lawful trading.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause stating that the agreement does not restrict securities trading creates a legal contradiction. According to the Law Explanation, the removal of this clause "opens the door for misinterpretation" and could "suggest that the NDA is intended to prevent legitimate trading activity." This directly relates to the prohibition of manipulative or deceptive devices as described in 15 USC 78j, which states "It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails...to use or employ any manipulative or deceptive device...in contravention of such rules and regulations." By removing the explicit allowance of securities trading, the modified NDA creates a potentially deceptive situation where lawful trading activity could be misconstrued as a violation of the NDA, contradicting the principles of fair trading practices protected under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934.
